nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reefs and open bottoms with rock, coral, sponge, or seawhip outcrops, typically encountered between about 10 and 50 m depth; juveniles have been documented sheltering in sponge areas under jetties.

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer a varied diet of Spirulina, nori and other marine algae, mysis shrimp, frozen meaty preparations, and high-quality angelfish formulas — ideally those containing sponge material, since wild adults have been documented feeding heavily on sponges and tunicates. Frequent small feedings throughout the day are commonly recommended, especially during acclimation.

summary: The False Personifer Angelfish (Chaetodontoplus meredithi) is a medium-large species from Australian waters, often mistaken for the closely related true Personifer Angelfish. Generally considered intermediate to keep, it tends to be semi-aggressive and is widely regarded as not reef-safe.
careNotes: The False Personifer Angelfish is endemic to the western Pacific around Queensland and northern New South Wales, Australia, and is occasionally exported through the marine aquarium trade. The species is sexually dimorphic — males have been reported to show a speckled yellow face and a slightly more elongate body, while females show a darker blue-green facial mask. Juveniles display a vibrant yellow body with vertical blue-and-white bars that become progressively less pronounced as the fish matures. Like other large Chaetodontoplus angels, it is generally considered intermediate to keep: it tends to need stable, mature water quality, large quantities of live rock arranged into caves and overhangs, and ample open swimming space. Newly imported wild specimens are commonly reported as shy and slow to accept prepared foods — sponge-enriched preparations alongside mysis, nori, and varied meaty foods tend to ease the transition, and feeding difficulty is generally regarded as moderate rather than easy. The species is widely considered not reef-safe, as many specimens have been reported to nip at stony and soft corals and clam mantles, though behaviour can vary between individuals. As a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, the dominant fish in a group will eventually transition from female to male; conspecific aggression toward other angelfish and similarly patterned Chaetodontoplus species tends to be high, so most keepers house a single specimen or a single confirmed pair. HLLE (head-and-lateral-line erosion) has been reported in poorly maintained large-angelfish systems, so stable water quality and a vitamin-rich, marine-algae-inclusive diet are generally advised.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely reported and is generally considered very challenging. As is typical of the angelfish family, the species spawns pelagically (releasing eggs and sperm into the open water column), and the resulting planktonic larvae tend to make larval rearing the limiting step for hobbyists attempting captive reproduction.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source for aquarium-trade husbandry: 180-gallon (~681 L) minimum, semi-aggressive temperament, not reef-safe, omnivore diet, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, temperature 22.2–25.6°C, 14" maximum size, sexual dimorphism (yellow-faced male vs blue-green-faced female).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes; Kuiter, 1990), max length (25 cm TL), depth range (10–50 m), wild temperature preference (23.9–27.6°C, mean ~26.1°C), Queensland-to-Lord-Howe-Island distribution, sponge-and-tunicate trophic level 3.0, and IUCN Least Concern status. Size disagreement with LiveAquaria (14" = ~36 cm vs 25 cm) was averaged to a 23–30 cm range.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank minimum (180 gal), temperature range (24–28°C), reef caution, intermediate care level, sexually dimorphic facial mask, juvenile vertical-bar color phase distinct from adults, and a reported lifespan of 10–15 years in captivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning and successful larval rearing have not been widely reported for the species; like other large angelfishes, pelagic eggs and planktonic larvae tend to make captive breeding very difficult.
